nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving and still freshwaters; floats on the surface of ponds, ditches, marshes and slow streams, often forming dense mats among other floating vegetation

decorPreferences: open water surface for floating mats
stones or driftwood with fishing line or mesh for tied carpets
moderate flow only — strong flow breaks up mats

goodWith: labyrinth fish that use floating cover
avoidWith: tanks with strong surface agitation that disperses mats

summary: Floating Crystalwort (Riccia fluitans) is a cosmopolitan aquatic liverwort with bright-green, forked, branching fronds. Commonly kept floating as a fry refuge, or tied down to stone with fishing line as a carpet — a technique popularized by Takashi Amano.
careNotes:
Floating Crystalwort is a liverwort — a group of primitive non-vascular plants (division Marchantiophyta) that predate true flowering plants — not a typical aquarium plant. It has no functional roots and absorbs water and nutrients directly through the surface of its flat, branching fronds (the thallus). This means substrate choice is essentially irrelevant; the plant floats or is attached to hardscape rather than planted in the ground.

It can be grown two ways, with quite different husbandry requirements. In its natural floating mode, Crystalwort forms dense, bright-green mats at the water surface, manages well under low to moderate light, does not require CO2 supplementation, and provides excellent shelter for fry and small shrimp. This is the easier approach and suits most community tanks.

The tied-carpet technique, popularized by the aquascape artist Takashi Amano in the early 1990s, involves bundling branches and tying them to stones or pieces of driftwood with fine fishing line, cotton thread, or stainless-steel mesh, then placing the anchored bundle on the substrate. Under good CO2 and medium light (around 0.5 W/L, with CO2 at 6–14 mg/L), the plant pearls visibly — releasing tiny oxygen bubbles from its fronds — and creates a lush foreground carpet. The challenge is that new shoots always grow toward the surface, so the mat tends to lift and float away over time, particularly under higher light and CO2. Keeping a tied carpet in place requires regular pruning back to short stubble and ongoing re-tying; it is a rewarding but maintenance-intensive approach.

In either mode, hair algae is the main threat — it easily takes hold in Riccia mats and is difficult to remove without dismantling the plant. Duckweed is another common contaminant that can outcompete Crystalwort on the surface if left unchecked. Strong surface agitation should be avoided, as it breaks up floating mats and stresses tied bundles.
breedingNotes: Propagation is straightforward. Breaking the thallus into smaller pieces and re-tying or re-floating them is all that is needed; the fragments establish readily. Riccia fluitans also releases spores from its branch tips that drift and establish new colonies on their own, which is one reason it can spread quickly in a tank. Tissue-culture cups are widely available from aquarium retailers and arrive pest- and algae-free; the gel medium should be rinsed away thoroughly before the plant is introduced to the aquarium.

primary.name: Tropica
primary.notes: Primary source for height (1–5 cm), light (medium, ~0.5 W/L), CO2 recommendation (6–14 mg/L), growth rate (medium), origin (cosmopolitan), and the Amano-popularized tied-carpet technique.

additional.name: Buce Plant
additional.notes: Cross-checked light (low to medium), CO2 (not necessary but recommended), pH (6–8), and both placement modes (free-floating mat vs attached to hardscape/mesh). Confirms tied carpets tend to grow upward and detach without pruning.

additional.name: Fish Laboratory
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (optimal 20–26 °C, tolerated 15–30 °C), pH (6.0–8.0), placement versatility, and Takashi Amano's 1990s popularization of submerged use.

additional.name: Aqua-fish.net
additional.notes: Cross-checked growth rate (3–5 cm per two months), light (moderate to high depending on placement), CO2 (6–14 mg/L for submerged carpet), and care level (easy in floating mode).

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Taxonomy source: family Ricciaceae, order Marchantiales, division Marchantiophyta (liverworts). Confirms cosmopolitan distribution, pond habitat, and competition pressure from duckweed and hair algae.
